name: SCA Dataset
description: Stochastic Cellular Automata (SCA) dataset for dual-rule identification and lambda estimation. Each sample is generated by interleaving two ECA rules (f and g) stochastically: at each cell and timestep, rule f is applied with probability lambda and rule g with probability (1-lambda). Features both orbit data and pre-computed statistics (frac_stats, within_var_stats) as model inputs. Training data is generated on-the-fly; only the test split is stored on disk.

generation.update_scheme: Cell-level stochastic: each cell independently applies rule f with probability lambda or rule g with probability (1-lambda) at each timestep.

wolfram_classes.A: Class I — fixed point / uniform (rules: 0,8,32,40,128,136,160,168 and equivalents)

pairs.description: Each sample is a (rule_f, rule_g, lambda) triple. Pairs are sampled to ensure coverage of all Wolfram class combinations (AxA, AxB, AxC, BxB, BxC, CxC).

field_descriptions.orbits: K independent orbit realizations per sample. Each orbit is [T, W] binary grid evolved under the stochastic rule mixture.
field_descriptions.frac_stats: 24 pre-computed fractional statistics across the K orbits (see compute_frac_stats in MODEL_SCA.py). Encodes per-pattern activation frequencies.
field_descriptions.within_var_stats: Per-orbit within-timestep variance (K values). Proxy for lambda*(1-lambda); used as an additional lambda estimator alongside frac_stats.
field_descriptions.rule_f_bits: 8-bit binary representation of rule f (LSB first). Stored as float32 for direct model input.
field_descriptions.rule_g_bits: 8-bit binary representation of rule g (LSB first). Stored as float32 for direct model input.
field_descriptions.lambdas: True lambda value used during simulation. Probability that rule f is applied at each cell/timestep.
field_descriptions.rule_f_ids: Integer rule number (0-255) for rule f.
field_descriptions.rule_g_ids: Integer rule number (0-255) for rule g.

SCA_Data/.train_pairs.npy: Array of shape [500, 2] — (rule_f, rule_g) training pairs
SCA_Data/.test_pairs.npy: Array of shape [500, 2] — (rule_f, rule_g) test pairs

loading_example.python:
import numpy as np
orbits    = np.load('SCA_Data/test/orbits.npy')
frac      = np.load('SCA_Data/test/frac_stats.npy')
wvar      = np.load('SCA_Data/test/within_var_stats.npy')
lambdas   = np.load('SCA_Data/test/lambdas.npy')
rule_f    = np.load('SCA_Data/test/rule_f_ids.npy')
rule_g    = np.load('SCA_Data/test/rule_g_ids.npy')

task.input: K orbits [N_ORBITS, T, W] + frac_stats [24] + within_var_stats [N_ORBITS]

targets.rule_f: 8-bit rule bits or integer ID for rule f
targets.rule_g: 8-bit rule bits or integer ID for rule g

ceiling_analysis.description: Information-theoretic signal analysis on the test set.
ceiling_analysis.signal_proxy: max(frac*(1-frac)) across 8 frac_stat channels — approximates tau*(1-tau) at disagreement patterns
ceiling_analysis.additional_signal: within_var_stats provides an independent lambda estimator (SCA advantage over TSCA)

categories.no_signal: max_frac_var < 0.01 — rules agree on all patterns; lambda and rules unrecoverable
categories.strong_signal: max_frac_var >= 0.05 — solvable with a good model

ceiling_analysis.notes: SCA is easier than TSCA because W independent Bernoulli draws per pattern per timestep (vs 1 binary draw per row in TSCA) yields cleaner frac estimates. within_var provides a second estimator of lambda*(1-lambda).

comparison_notes.vs_TSCA: Same rule split (SEED=42), same pair sampling strategy and Wolfram class coverage. SCA uses cell-level lambda mixing (easier); TSCA uses row-level tau switching (harder). SCA adds within_var_stats as an extra feature.
comparison_notes.vs_ECANet_New: Different task structure — SCA identifies a pair of rules and a mixing probability, whereas ECANet-New identifies a single rule from a single orbit.
